q: Rate is a measure of how fast or how slow the reaction is.
To describe the quantitative measurement of a reaction rate, it is necessary to specify how fast the reactants are used up or the products are formed.
An iron nail reacts quickly in the air as it rusts.
White phosphorus bursts into flames when exposed to air.
Candle wax burns only after the wick of the candle is lighted.
The rate of reaction remains constant for different reactions and under different conditions.
The rate of reaction means measuring the change in the amount of a product produced or a reactant used up in the time taken for that change.
The reaction between magnesium ribbon and hydrochloric acid gives off oxygen gas.
Magnesium reacts with dilute hydrochloric acid giving off hydrogen gas according to the following chemical equation: $Mg (s) + 2HCl (aq) \longrightarrow MgCl_2 (aq) + H_2 (g)$.
The rate of reaction can be expressed as the change in the amount (mass, volume or concentration) of reactants or products per unit of time.
If the substance is a gas, the amount consumed or formed is expressed in pressure units or as measured in volume.
Concentration units are used if the reactant or product is in solution.
Time measurement is expressed in whatever units fit the reaction (seconds, minutes, hours, days, months, or years).

Generally, in chemical reactions, the rate of a reaction in solution is measured in terms of a decrease in the concentration of the reactant or an increase in the concentration of the product per unit time at a constant temperature.
The minus (-) sign in the rate expression means an increase in the concentration of the reactant.
The plus (+) sign in the rate expression means an increase in the concentration of the product.
Rate is expressed as a positive quantity.
The rate constant, $k$, explains the relationship between the molar concentration of the reactants and the rate of a chemical reaction.
The mole ratios from the balanced chemical equation can be used to determine the rates of change in concentrations of products and reactants.
The unit for a reaction rate depends only on the physical states of reactants.
Explosion of dynamite takes within a second.
Weathering of rocks happens within a second.
Depletion of the ozone layer depends on the relative rates of reactions that produce and destroy ozone molecules.
The study of reaction rates is known as "chemical kinetics".
Enzyme action is essential in food preservatives.
The economical synthesis of ammonia to produce fertiliser depends on the rate at which gaseous nitrogen and hydrogen can be converted to ammonia.
Knowledge of reaction rates is required in drug design, pollution control, and food processing.
Reaction time is directly proportional to the rate.
The unit for the rate of a chemical reaction in solution is $mol\ dm^{-3}\ s^{-1}$.
Rusting of an iron pipe happens over months or years.
Collision theory explains how chemical reactions occur and is based on the kinetic molecular theory of gases.
Reactant particles moving in random directions possess kinetic energy.
When particles frequently collide with one another, the energy is transferred as a result of the collision.
All collisions between reactant particles result in the formation of reaction products.
For a reaction to occur, colliding particles must impact with certain minimum energy known as an activation energy.
The spatial orientation of the reactant molecules is important to determine whether or not a reaction occurs.
If the colliding particles have the incorrect orientation, they will bounce off each other.
The unsuccessful collisions with insufficient energy do not produce a reaction.
Successful collision (effective collision) occurs when particles collide with the correct orientation and have enough energy to produce a reaction.
The rate of reaction depends on the rate of successful collisions between reactant particles.
Reacting particles must collide with sufficient energy to form the activated complex.
The activated complex is a permanent species formed by the reactant particles as a result of the collision before they form the product.
The more successful collisions there are, the more the activated complex leads to the rate of a reaction being faster.
Reacting particles (atoms, molecules, or ions) must collide in the correct orientation with sufficient energy to overcome the energy barrier to react.
A reaction will speed up if the frequency of collision increases.
A reaction will speed up if the proportion of particles with energy that is greater than the activation energy increases.

In terms of the collision theory, for a chemical reaction to occur, the colliding molecules must possess at least a certain minimum energy necessary to rearrange the original bonds breaking or new bonds forming.
The activation energy ($E_a$) is the minimum energy that reactant particles must possess for a successful collision to take place in a reaction.
During the progress of a reaction, an unstable intermediate known as an activated complex is formed before producing a product.
In order to get the product, the reactants must pass through a short-lived, high-energy transition state.
The minimum energy that needs to be reached in the transition state is equal to the activation energy ($E_a$) for that reaction.
The activation energy represents an energy barrier for the reaction and has the same value in all chemical reactions.
In an exothermic reaction, the final product has less energy than that of the reactants.
If the energy is not enough, it cannot overcome the energy barrier and the decomposition of the product, that is, back to reactants will occur.
A high $E_a$ means that relatively many collisions have the required energy to produce the activated complex.
With a low $E_a$, more collisions have sufficient energy to react, and the reaction rate is fast.
Successful collision is a collision that causes a chemical reaction.
Transition state (also known as an intermediate state) is a highly energetic state that would be highly unstable.
The activated complex is a temporary unstable arrangement of atoms that may form products or break apart to reform the reactants.
Collision theory states that a chemical reaction only takes place when two reacting molecules collide in the correct orientation with sufficient kinetic energy.
Increasing the concentrations of the reactants increases the rate of reactions for many reactions involving liquids or gases.
If more molecules or ions of the reactants are in the reaction area, then there is a greater chance of more effective collisions per unit time.
Zinc granule reacts much faster with concentrated $6.0\ mol\ dm^{-3}$ hydrochloric acid than with $1.0\ mol\ dm^{-3}$ dilute hydrochloric acid.
In reactions involving solids, a reaction occurs at the boundary surface between reactants.
The rate of a reaction involving a solid reactant is decreased by increasing the surface area of the solid.
The smaller the particle size, the larger the surface area will be.
If the surface area is larger, it will be hit by more moving reacting particles per unit time, and the rate of reaction will be faster.
Aluminium foil reacts moderately with sodium hydroxide solution only when warmed.
Powdered aluminium of the same mass reacts rapidly in the cold with sodium hydroxide solution.
Large pieces of wood are difficult to ignite whereas small pieces burn more rapidly.
Changing the pressure does not affect the rate of reaction that only involves solids or liquids.
Increasing the pressure increases the speed of a reaction involving gases.
The pressure is directly proportional to the volume of a gas at a constant temperature.
At low pressure, the volume becomes larger, the number of collisions of gaseous molecules is smaller, and thus, the rate of reaction is slower.
At high pressure, the volume becomes smaller, the number of collisions is greater, and thus, the rate of reaction is faster.
Food can cook faster in a pressure cooker than in a pot because trapped steam becomes hotter as the pressure increases.
Most chemical reactions react faster at a low temperature than at a high temperature.
The kinetic energy of a gas is directly proportional to the temperature.
Increasing the temperature increases the kinetic energy, which means molecules move faster and collide more frequently with sufficient activation energy.
The number of effective collisions per second of the reacting particles increases as temperature increases.
Foods are cooled or frozen to speed up the chemical reactions that result in the spoiling of food.
The effect of temperature on reaction rates can also be explained by using the Boltzmann distribution curve.
The area under the Boltzmann distribution curve represents the number of particles within a certain energy range.
Chemical reactions take place only if the collided particles possess activation energy, $E_a$.
When the temperature is increased from $T_1$ to $T_2$, the shape of the distribution curve remains unchanged.
Generally, for a ten degrees rise in temperature, the rate of reactions approximately doubles.
A catalyst is defined as a substance which alters the rate of a chemical reaction but remains chemically changed at the end of the reaction.
Positive catalysts make chemical reactions go faster.
Iron is used as a positive catalyst in the Haber Process for manufacturing ammonia.
Vanadium(V) oxide or platinised asbestos is used in the Contact Process for manufacturing sulphur trioxide.
Manganese(IV) oxide is used in the preparation of oxygen from hydrogen peroxide.
A negative catalyst is used when a chemical reaction needs to go more slowly or to suppress unwanted reactions.
Alcohol acts as a negative catalyst to slow down the oxidation of sodium sulphite to sodium sulphate.
Catalysts provide an alternative pathway for the reaction with lower activation energy.
The rate of catalysed reaction is faster than that of uncatalysed reaction.
Homogeneous catalysts are in a different phase from the reactants in a catalysed reaction.
Heterogeneous catalysts are in a different phase from the reactants in a catalysed reaction.
Most heterogeneous catalysts are solids that catalyse either gaseous or liquid reactants.
In the ammonia oxidation process (Ostwald process), Pt-Rh catalyst speeds up the reaction.
Enzymes act as biocatalysts for specific biochemical reactions in living systems.
Ptyalin in saliva and pepsin in gastric juice are digestive enzymes that hasten the breakdown of large molecules.
The reaction rate using enzymes is much slower than inorganic catalysts.
Reactions that are initiated by light are called photochemical reactions.
The brighter the light, the faster the photochemical reaction.
Photosynthesis in green plants is an example of a photochemical reaction.
Decomposition of silver bromide in photographic films is not a photochemical reaction.
The reaction between hydrogen and chlorine is extremely slow in the dark but becomes explosive in intense sunlight via a chain reaction.
Phase is a portion of a physical state that is homogeneous throughout and can be separated physically from other phases.
When large amounts of flammable powdered substances collect in grain elevators or coal mines, violent explosions can occur.
Storage of preserved foods at a sub-zero temperature in a refrigerator is effective for slowing the rate of bacterial decay.
At high altitudes where the temperature is less than $100\ ^{\circ}C$, water boils at $100\ ^{\circ}C$.
Corrosion inhibitors like imidazoline and quaternary amine prevent $H_2S$ and $CO_2$ corrosion.
Volatile corrosion inhibitors (VCI) such as morpholine or hydrazine prevent corrosion in boilers.
Wrapping metals in volatile corrosion inhibitor-coated paper is a common long-term protection against moisture.
